sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 54, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 53 actions; a strong score needs about 28 or fewer. You are 1.9x over the target.
If you are not steadily making progress toward the level goal, commit to your single best hypothesis and the shortest sequence that tests it — do not exhaustively scan rows/columns or enumerate every option. Test one idea, read the result, then decide.

sections.content:
Excellent! Now I see the right green square has moved to (31, 40), which is inside the right frame area! The right frame is at (28, 46), and the right green square is at (31, 40), which is to the left of the right frame. Looking at the ASCII:
Row 30: `wwwwwwwwNNNwwwwwwwwwwwwppppppNNNwwcccccw`
Row 31: `wwwwwwwwNWNwwwwwwwwwwwwppppppNcNwwwwwwww`
Row 32: `wcccccwwNNNwwwwwwwwwwwwppppppNNNwwwwwwww`
The right green square is at cols 39-41 (center 40), and the right frame border is at cols 44-48. The right green square is to the left of the right frame border.
So the RIGHT action moved the right green square from (31, 22) all the way to (31, 40), passing through the purple bar! This is a big jump - the right green square moved 18 cells to the right in one action.
Wait, that's strange. Each RIGHT action should move the green square 3 cells. But the right green square moved from (31, 22) to (31, 40), which is 18 cells. That's 6 RIGHT actions worth of movement in one action.
Actually, looking more carefully, the right green square jumped from (31, 22) to (31, 40), which is exactly at the position where it would be if it moved RIGHT 6 times. But I only pressed RIGHT once. So maybe the RIGHT action moves the green square all the way to the right until it hits an obstacle.
Wait, but the right green square is at (31, 40), and the right frame border is at cols 44-48. So the right green square didn't hit the right frame border. Maybe the right green square moved to the right edge of the right frame area, which is at col 42 or so.
Actually, looking at the ASCII, the right green square is at cols 39-41 (NNN), and the right frame border is at cols 44-48 (ccccc). So there's a gap of 2 cells (cols 42-43) between the right green square and the right frame border.
Hmm, but the right green square is at (31, 40), which is inside the right frame area. The right frame is at (28, 46), which is the center of the right frame. The right frame border is at cols 44-48, rows 26-30.
Wait, I'm getting confused. Let me re-examine the right frame.
The right frame has:
- c border: px=16, center=(28, 46), boundary=[[26, 44], [26, 48], [30, 48], [30, 44]]
- w inside: px=9, center=(28, 46), boundary=[[27, 45], [27, 47], [29, 47], [29, 45]]
So the right frame is at rows 26-30, cols 44-48. The white square inside is at rows 27-29, cols 45-47.
The right green square is at (31, 40), which is at rows 30-32, cols 39-41. This is below and to the left of the right frame.
So the right green square is NOT inside the right frame. It's to the left of the right frame and below it.
Looking at the ASCII:
Row 30: `wwwwwwwwNNNwwwwwwwwwwwwppppppNNNwwcccccw`
Row 31: `wwwwwwwwNWNwwwwwwwwwwwwppppppNcNwwwwwwww`
Row 32: `wcccccwwNNNwwwwwwwwwwwwppppppNNNwwwwwwww`
The right green square is at cols 39-41, and the right frame border is at cols 44-48. So the right green square is to the left of the right frame.
But wait, the right frame is at rows 26-30, and the right green square is at rows 30-32. So the right green square is below the right frame (row 30 is the bottom of the right frame, and the right green square starts at row 30).
Hmm, so the right green square is at the bottom-left corner of the right frame area. It's not inside the right frame.
OK, so the RIGHT action moved the right green square from (31, 22) to (31, 40). This is a big jump. Let me think about what happened.
Actually, I think the RIGHT action moves the green square all the way to the right until it hits an obstacle. The right green square was at (31, 22), and it moved RIGHT until it hit the right frame border at col 44. But the right green square is 3 cells wide, so it would stop at col 41 (44 - 3 = 41), which is where it is now (center 40, so cols 39-41).
Wait, but the right frame border is at cols 44-48, and the right green square is at cols 39-41. So there's a gap of 2 cells (cols 42-43) between them. Maybe the right green square stopped because it hit the right frame's white square or something.
Actually, looking at the ASCII, the right frame border is at cols 44-48 on row 30, but on row 31, the right frame area is empty (wwwwwwww). So the right green square at (31, 40) is not blocked by the right frame border.
Hmm, maybe the right green square moved to the right edge of the right frame area, which is at col 42 or so. But the right green square is at col 40, which is 2 cells to the left of col 42.
Actually, I think the right green square moved to the right until it hit the right edge of the right frame's interior area. The right frame's interior is at cols 45-47, and the right green square is at cols 39-41. So the right green square is 3 cells to the left of the right frame's interior.
Wait, I'm overcomplicating this. Let me just try to move the right green square UP to get it into the right frame.
Currently, the right green square is at (31, 40). The right frame is at rows 26-30, cols 44-48. To get the right green square into the right frame, I need to move it UP and RIGHT.
But first, let me check if the right green square can move UP. The right green square is at row 31, and the right frame is at rows 26-30. So I need to move the right green square UP to row 28 (the center of the right frame).
But to move the right green square, I need to press UP/DOWN/LEFT/RIGHT. Currently, the left green square is at (31, 19), and the right green square is at (31, 40). When I press RIGHT, the right green square moves. So maybe when the left green square is at (31, 19), LEFT/RIGHT controls the right green square.
Let me try pressing UP to see if the right green square moves UP.

sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Right green square is at (31, 40), need to move it into the right frame at (28, 46)
# Try UP to move the right green square up
action(['UP'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
